summary: The post argues that the artificial divide between software engineering and data teams is outdated and counterproductive, especially as data becomes increasingly integrated into applications through ML/AI and streaming. Reis observes that while 'shit flows downhill' from dev to data teams in traditional org structures, this separation will inevitably disappear as data becomes the application itself.
key_points: Software engineering and data teams are often siloed even at ML-focused companies
Data has evolved beyond one-way reporting flows to becoming integrated into applications
Forces like data products, data mesh, and data-centric models will accelerate the convergence
The traditional reporting-only mindset is increasingly obsolete
money_quotes: Engineering throws stuff to the data team, expecting the data team to deal with it. As the old saying goes, shit flows downhill, in this case, to the data teams.
The ubiquity of ML/AI, streaming data, reverse ETL (I wish this term would disappear), and other feedback loops back to the application mean data isn't something that lives outside the application.
Inevitably, the software and data divide will disappear.
key_insight: The traditional organizational separation between software engineering and data teams is becoming obsolete as data moves from being a byproduct of applications to being the core of the application itself.
